author: 👑 of Puts
content: Can u explain you strategy please? Where would your SL be and where would you take profit? Also if you're trading B&R how would you qualify your first trade? And do you normally trade on 2 min or 1 min?

author: PlugSabe
content: Yeah so I only trade the B/R. I always wait for a signal candle and my SL will go slightly above the high of the candle. On the first trade I just targeted a 2:1 R and the second trade I targeted LOD which was the same level. I consider the first trader to be a B/R because it closed and held below the PML then failed to close back above on that big wick. I watch the 1min for an entry first, but if the candle closes above the level, then I watch to see if the 2min confirms which is wat happened in this case. Iv backtested the 1 and 2 min and for my strategy they have the same winrate, I just take whichever confirms.

author: 👑 of Puts
content: How i would trade this is: on 1hr i'd look for key levels (923-920/892) then trade on lower time frame. In my opinion this was nice B&R on 1 hr. So if i was looking for calls i'd take PML with a target 920 and a play down from the top would be what i'd really look for since i'm looking for retest of that breakout area.

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content:
Thanks for the kind words! Let me break this down for you:

Why the stop loss on the second break, not the first:
First Candle's Wick Touches the Trendline:

The first candle's wick tested the trendline but did not decisively break it. This can often be interpreted as a false breakdown or a trendline retest.
The wick bouncing off the trendline can indicate indecision, and the trendline still holds as valid support.
Second Candle's Clear Break and Close Below:

The second candle closed below the trendline with no part of the body touching the trendline or 9 EMA. This signifies that the trendline has been broken decisively.
A candle closing below both the trendline and the 9 EMA is a strong bearish signal, warranting an exit to safeguard capital.

Why Hold Through the First Test:
Holding through the first test can be strategic because trendlines often get retested multiple times. Selling too early might lead to missing a potential bounce or continuation in your favor.
The key is to wait for confirmation of the break, which comes when a candle closes below the trendline (with no touch or retest) and undercuts additional support like the 9 EMA.

When to Sell All Contracts:
The trendline break combined with the close below the 9 EMA signals a shift in momentum.
The decision to sell all contracts was based on the lack of support from both the trendline and the EMA, indicating that the bullish thesis no longer held.

I'm also interested in this question. In your case, it's still a decent entry because there's still a good distance to the low of the day. But sometimes, due to a large test candle, there's very little left to the low of the day, with a profit potential of around R1.6.

On one hand, a strong candle could push further than the LOD, but in practice, that rarely happens for me.

Morning Chop: Multiple BNRs failed, no clean setups, stayed out.
Entry: Short after break & retest of key level.
Trend Confirmation: Strong rejection, followed the downtrend.

✅ Trendline guided trade – locked profits before reversal.

author: Tito Frescado
content:
Overview:
FOMC day but strong uptrend on SPY and QQQ. I thought that since AMZN was above YDH so early in the day with a strong uptrend behind it on all timeframes that it might continue. AMZN was above YDH and retested with a strong hammer candle with long bottom wick. Entered on the close of the second hammer candle with long bottom wick, looking for the daily gap fill. Exited when a candle closed below the first order bock. 

Setup Details:
Entry: FOMC day but strong uptrend on SPY and QQQ. AMZN showed a strong uptrend on all timeframes. AMZN was above YDH and retested with a strong hammer candle with long bottom wick. Entered on the close of the second hammer candle with long bottom wick, looking for the daily gap fill.
Position Size: Discuss the position size taken and why. Mention any adjustments made for risk management.
Exit: Exited when a candle closed below the first order bock. 
Profit/Loss: Lost 20 dollars on the trade. On second look, the was perhaps too optimistic trade on FMOC day when you expect the trade to stay within the PDR. Perhaps my entry was too aggressive because although the hammer was red and might have worked on another day, it was FMOC day. I should have waited for a green confirmation candle close above the red hammer that I entered on. It would have kept me out of the trade.

Rating: ⭐ [5 of Stars]
⭐ 1 Star: Although the setup was good. I should have waited for more confirmation that it wanted to continue above YDR.
Key Takeaways:
Lesson 1: Expect price to stay within YDR on FOMC day before 2:00. Wait for price to reach YDH or YDL looking for a quick scalp in the opposite direction
Lesson 2: Look for solid confirmation before entering a trade on FMOC day.
Missed Opportunities: No. the was a retest of the top of the daily gap but not enough room to move before hitting resistance at YDH.
Other Factors: FOMC

author: Kam
content:
Hi Chris — appreciate you sharing this one, solid context and nice work calling out the thesis. A few nuances you might find helpful on review:

1. Context check – NVDA was trading inside the prior day’s range, so it’s a tougher environment for longs unless the setup is really clean. In those conditions, any play often needs to be treated as a scalp unless something really breaks open.

2. Initial strength – The push above OR was nice, but keep an eye on the repeated upper wicks near 143.14 (prior pivot from yesterday). That level seemed to act as early resistance.

3. Trigger candle sequence – The 9:45 hammer is a good eye, but watch how 9:46 fails to follow through — another upper wick and no strong body. That’s often a red flag in this kind of setup. Remember what Tony/Jdub say about “want to see strong PA here” — that second candle needs to prove it.

4. Opening wick awareness – That big wick on the opening candle turned nito OB is worth clocking. Even though the 9:35 hammer dug into it, it wouldn’t be unreasonable for price to come all the way back toward 142.50 and still be structurally intact. That should factor into your risk and target structure.

Hope this is helpful!

author: Kam
content:
Kuba - first off, solid work on the entries across the board. You’re reading levels really well and triggering on logical reclaim/retest sequences. Nicely done!

The challenge, as you already know, isn’t setup quality - it’s your exit conviction.  I totally get that the “1 con” situation definitely makes things feel more fragile, but something to consider from these trades is that perhaps your trade management is reacting to price flickers, not to your original plan or RR zones. Hindsight is always 20/20 but perhaps something to ask yourself is how would I have handled scales if I had 2-3 cons?

author: Kam
content:
I think these are further evidence you’re not missing the setups. You’re not even mistiming entries. You’re simply not giving these trades the room to breathe through your target zones. And while the 1-con constraint makes that harder, the deeper issue may be that your exit logic is driven by discomfort rather than invalidation.

All three trades had valid structure. All three showed follow-through. And all three were closed early out of fear. If you want to level up without changing your contract size, consider defining hard structural targets ahead of time - and only exit if those are invalidated.

Keep working on the exits (this is probably the weakest part of my game also) Your entries and eye for structure look fantastic!

author: Ruslan /ruˈslan/
content:
Hi Kuba, I think I have the same issue as you, and maybe my experience can help. I also trade with just one contract (due to a small account) and often end up with small profits because I chickened out and exited too early — or I hesitated to sell, price dropped, and I took a loss instead of securing a small green.
I asked <@1014389514141184074> for advice on the best strategy for trading with a single contract — maybe it'll help you too. He said: “Set your TP at 1:1.5 RR, and once price goes past it, turn that level into your stop-loss and see if it continues.” Though I see you're already going for 1:2 RR, which is great.
But here's something I realized this past week: it’s not really about  "chickened out" vs "had the balls", it’s about trading a single contract with no ability to scale out, and I'll explain why. Despite my small account, I decided to try trading two contracts this week — and man, it’s a whole different game. Trading before and after this shift feels like night and day.
That inner conflict is gone — no more one voice saying “hold for more profit” while the other says “just take what you can before it turns red.” Now I calmly wait for TP1, take partial profit, or get stopped out — either way, it feels in control. And when you lock in some gains at TP1, it’s so much easier to let the rest ride toward TP2 with a peaceful mind.
Another great tip I got from <@801169196015943731> — which I now use — is after scaling out at TP1, move your stop to breakeven. That way you protect your profits and keep your risk low.
Sure, sometimes price reverses right away and it’s a small red — but I liked what <@1014389514141184074> said yesterday on trade review — that’s still a win. Because you followed your plan, didn’t lose control, and didn’t blow half your account 😄 We're not aiming for a 100% win rate anyway.
So my advice: try adding that second contract. It changes everything.

author: Kam
content:
Hey Jatin - this is a tricky one for me since it's lower timeframe bearish, but HTF bullish. I didn't take this trade, but here's how I hypothetically would have managed it:

The 155 area was the reclaim level after the 5m hammer into the PDH level so once we get a candle close above that level I'd draw out the full OB and use that as structural invalidation. I'd have taken profit like you at HOD. That nasty wick held that OB - and after taking profits at HOD, the trade is de-risked so if it comes back it comes back, and stop out for BE or small loss depending on your size. I probably would have sold into new high pops at 156.5, but these setups usually work fast and hit PMH quickly. So once it stalled out, I probably would have been cautious when got back under the HOD pivot and 1m/5m OB to stop out of trailers. 

There's different ways to trail though - I tend to use OB's and structure to trail, but others like <@1121621751764369501> use trendlines and EMAs. <@1014389514141184074> uses a 5m timer! Just have to find your style!

Easy to play armchair QB with hindsight bias i know 😂  But that's my 2c, thanks for asking - this was a good thought exercise!

I know Tony & Jack took this trade cos they were just talking about it in Traders Lab - so definitely check out Tony's trade review of this tonight!

author: 305 Trader
content: Here is a trick I apply. I do not enter at or near HOD or LOD. I wait for the price to retrace back to a POI, and then on  the B&R of my POI in continuation to the market direction, I take an entry, then my 1st TP will HOD or LOD, and let it ride.

author: Papa
content: In general there should have been more displacement off of the PMH level for it to be considered a higher probability retest, expect reaction there with sellers which is what happened.  The entry for retest would have been a better entry off the first 5 min high hammer candle for low risk, 1 min chart has multiple one's there with buyers stepping in.  Also something to keep in mind, the 5 min chart shows 3 green bars, that kind of move usually requires a retracement, things like to move in 3's.  The reclaim of the PMH was an aggressive entry, would have been better to wait for HOD B/R with displacement to confirm continuation higher.  Hope this helps <@1070052905522053272>

author: Dcrouth21
content: I think you did a great job of scaling. Looks like you had targets set and had the discipline to wait for them to get hit. I think with 6 cons you scaled very well. I am not saying to size up but if you had more cons you could have scaled 50% at tp1, 25% at tp2, and 10-15% at tp3 leaving some trailers for continuation. I find it tricky sometimes to scale with smaller amounts of cons as in your case you could only scale 16-17% if you took off 1 con at tp2 or 33% if you took off 2 cons. Based on the current market conditions I would say it was much better to take off 2 cons at tp2 than 1 especially considering that it seems you were already at a 2:1 RR. It is also important to note that it is not about the number of cons that you take or the PNL that is important its about focusing on the process. If you had cons that you left for continuation I would scale at whole psych numbers or highs on the daily chart that have not been swept. As for cutting trailers you could bar by bar it on the 5 min or wait for it to break 5 min structure. Overall if I were you I would be pretty happy with those executions

author: FierceTiger

author: FierceTiger
content: Premarket high gets taken out, I short when market structure is confirmed, then I exit when momentum stops. You can see my earlier comments about premarket high for reference. I do actually trade this setup regularly.

author: FierceTiger
content: I entered as soon as I confirmed CISD on the 2 minute TF. My point of invalidation was high of day. Note that this is an A+ setup. Accumulation and manipulation was very clear from the pre-market - I only caught the distribution leg and rode it on the way down. Once this setup is formed, the chances of it reversing and stopping me out is extremely low. Also, note that you usually don't get a PO3 every time in the premarket with the premarket high getting taken out on the first few candles at market open. Sometimes PM high does not get taken out and it goes down anyway (this would not be A+, as it can reverse anytime). However once the PO3 short setup with PM high taken out presents itself, it is a bread-and-butter A+ setup.

author: Anne
content:
Very bad day today regarding PnL (-1988$), good day regarding "respect the process". I saw the trades through until they hit SL. Than I added a fourth trade only because I am still on paper and I want to use this state to try out what comes up in my brain. As I am a very bad rule holder I decided last week to implement only one rule after the other: First I did risk managment (don't stay in the trade after candle close beneath SL), next is: only exit when SL or PT is hit. In other words: don't look at the PnL, look at the process. Well, today that wasn't a nice thing to do but I don't feel really bad with such a loss on paper anymore. That means I don't identify my personal value with the outcome of a trade anymore. 
The fourth trade I took was out of the "take only three trades a day" rule but I allowed it due to that I am still on paper and I did not implement that rule yet. That rule will be the last one that I will implement while being on paper trading. The most important rule for me now is: Stay in the trade until it hits PT. My wining stats are good, I just exited too early up to now because I rather took a few dollars win then loosing a trade. This is going to change!

author: Kam
content:
Hey Sean, I think this question somewhat depends on how you saw the setup / entry. I'm guessing you were trying the retest off the HOD / 5m ORH? If it were me, I'd have drawn out the full 1m OB since we had more displacement from there and structurally is the area price could dig back into. From my perspective the ideal entry on this trade is the reclaim of that OB /HOD on the 9:45 candle with a stop below the full OB. 

There wasn't really any 'sequence' on the pullback on your entry, more of an immediate retest - I don't usually take those trades but if I did I'd probably manage it more aggressively with a break below the retest level; one of those - 'has to work right away' situations IMO. 

But I think you have it right - structurally the invalidation looks to me is below the low of that 9:33 downclose candle

author: 305 Trader
content:
<@975051189316100096> Hi Chen. I thought I could share a thought on your trade. When taking a put or a call, wait for the set up that offers a larger displacement prior, so that your initial RvR is 1:2. 
I drew in  a blue box your relatively short displacement. 
Then in your 2nd entry for another put I guess, look at the strength of that pullback. Massive green candles that took out sellers. They even formed a higher high that took out your previous higher high which needed to be respected for the downward trend not to have a ChOCH. 
So your 2nd entry had very low prob of win and the fact that it worked it was to exit quick at LOD or the lower low of that dump.

author: Kam
content:
https://discord.com/channels/1218766394997346395/1219022389396901958/1427819217780211732 <@648855504994566154> moving the AMD trade discussion to this channel. Attaching some markup also in case it helps. There were a couple of things that kept me out earlier (I'm assuming you entered at 10:42?). 

1) QQQ context. Q's were struggling at PDL and I wanted to see a reclaim of PDL and ideally PMH for any longs at that point. AMD also wasn't moving as strongly as the indices (QQQ was well above HOD and AMD was barely trying to break above)

2) After 10:15 EST I stop looking at the 1m chart for entries and have a rule to only take 5m candle closures - especially on reclaims. 

3) Something Tony has drilled into my head - "where does the momo start"? For AMD my PM plan was to look for long setups over PMH with the gap-up. We weren't at PMH so started looking at intraday stucture for momo evidence. Since we hadn't displaced above HOD level (other than PM), 10:42 was more of a breakout trade to me (vs a reclaim). When the 5m didn't close above HOD in the morning session - the wicks from those attempts at 222 became the momentum level to me where buyers likely trapped 

4) Set an alert at 222 and only entered when 5m closed above that HOD pivot - note the QQQ context was much better at noon EST also (Above all intraday pivots and trending). Because AMD had attempted to reclaim at 11:40 but gave those trap wicks, once we closed above 222 had even more conviction that could push to PMH / PDH

5) Side-note - for a reclaim trade I'd still say this was a B-grade setup because of the lack of an impulse candle on the 5m, and relative weakness to QQQ - sort of trusted the HTFs and all the other things mentioned 

Sorry for all the detail - helps me walk back through it also, so I appreciate the question!

author: 305 Trader
content:
I wanted to share how I am trading the indices in this ATH levels with no references.

- If trading before 10:45 EST which I do not do, use Fibonacci extensions. I am observing they are respected. 

- Trading only after 11 am to allow for intraday key levels in 10, 15 and 30 mins. 

- No FOMO. Do not try to ride a move already started regardless of how much momentum. 

- Wait for the pullback back to a Key Level you trust and from there observe whst direction the market takes in a “most conservative” type of confirmation especially if you trade in 1 mjn TF where lot of false signals are occuring. 

- Avoid taking an entry at or near HOD. Obvious.

author: 305 Trader
content:
hi Adil. I would try avoiding entering at LOD if this was LOD?? altogether or nearby. You can always expect a pullback there which in this case its length was luckily into your marked FVG. How did you determine your reward? Green vs red looks it was just a 1:1.4 RvR. This could be an elevated risk to take. 
Its always advisable to wait for the pullback from HOD or LOD and then watch the reaction at the end of it. Also is the top of the pullback respecting the previous Lower High ? In this case it did. So this is why it worked for you.

Your second trade was valid, but you would have needed to get in before that big red candle at 9:46 closed because if you enter near the bottom of that candle, your risk is very high and your R:R doesn't make too much sense, especially if you're playing 0DTEs. author: Kam
content:
hey Dane, I didn't take this but was watching it (short side is not my best side to put it mildly lol)

I'd suggest drawing out market structure (orderblocks) or HTF levels rather than just using the 5m level as your stop. 421.87 was last Fri low, and if you draw out at least the wick to the body portion of the 9:31 candle you can see the confluence. 

A slightly wider stop above structure is probably the best adjustment on your trade, which may mean smaller size for the wider risk. 

Your entry was a little aggressive given the lack of displacement below LOD, but that's actually ok because you generally have to be more aggressive on the short side because the flushes may not give multiple entry opps like on the long side. 

I was personally looking at the 9:41 candle break but I blinked 😩

author: Das Wookie
content: Fifth day of posting my daily trade results... I managed to capture a new daily record for myself. I hope everybody else did well today!

author: Das Wookie
content:
I broke a number of my rules today with my trades... not all of my rules, but quite a bunch of them:
- Don't trade after 11 - I couldn't resist the afternoon downturn to go short on PLTR and GOOG
- Don't revenge trade - I lost my first entry trying a trade with RGTI, so I exited the position, played a Uno Reverse card on my position and immediately re-entered, and lost again! -$4k on both. Doggie! That sucked!
- Don't chase! DON'T F-ING CHASE! Got it! - See Revenge / RGTI trade
- Don't force trades - See Revenge / RGTI trade
- Sit on your hands and WAIT for your entry - See Revenge / RGTI trade
- Don't flit from one trade to another - See Revenge / RGTI trade
- Max of 3 daily options trades... I entered in on 11 trades in total today
- Don't over trade - See max 3 trades
- If I do well on either of my initial 2 trades, and I'm green for the day... you're done! - I only had the two trades on TSLA and INTC, but I got greedy and wanted more. It worked out, but it shouldn't have. I got lucky that it worked out, but I SHOULD have just taken the initial win and shut down for the day and protected my wins. With the RGTI trade, I nearly went negative on all of my initial TSLA trade gains
- Capture gains at key levels and level out of position! - I ended up losing a potential $33k because I didn't take profit at a key level
- Don't move your stop! didn't exit at my initial stop, and then moved my stop, twice, trying to recapture the potential unrealized gain instead of just capturing the trade where my initial stop was set.

author: Das Wookie
content:
Generally, yeah, I'm striving for max profits, but...

I'll scale out if it's a scalp trade and I'm not looking to play it out past a pullback. I'll also level out if I hit a key level if I'm in it for longer and am willing to sit through a pullback... sometimes I'll scale out some, other times I'll hold but be ready to VERY quickly dump the trade if it looks like a reversal. Some of it's just based on my thesis for the trade and if I have a reason to believe that the trade hasn't played out yet. Sometimes that means I end up making less because I might have leveled out earlier or exited the trade.

There's so many factors I'm considering at that exact moment I would be incapable of even enumerating them all... Basically tho, I'm boiling down to one of three thoughts:
- SCRAM the trade! It's reversed! GET OUT! NOW!!!
- Scale out most of our profits / hold some trailers, because the magic 8-Ball says: ¯\_(ツ)_/¯
- We're Too Close. Stay on Target. Lighten up! Stay On Target!!!

author: Das Wookie
content:
I have made my own rules, and a lot of my Options Trading rules differs from my Shares Trading rules. This is because with shares, you can still buy when volume is only 3,000 a minute. You can also trade pretty much everything, where as with options... No Effin Way!!! The 11am rule is there for a liqudity and volume reason, but if both are still there, party on. In GENERAL tho, once you are into intraday trading, it gets SIGNIFICANTLY harder! Today was a fluke.... and a 22-point drop in the Q's was a VERY target rich environment. GOOG and PLTR was where I went, but you could have just as easily gone with SQQQ, AMD, AAPL, MSFT, TSLA, AMZN, NVDA, NFLX, META, ORCL, MU, MSTR, AVGO, INTC, COIN, CRWV, HOOD, OKLO, RKLB, SOFI, TSM... everything was in freefall. Really, gains this afternoon was pretty much limited only by ones capital and bandwidth to throw at the trades. I can't personally manage more than 2 trades at a time. Well maybe if I threw a follow stop at it, and gave myself something like a $1 pullback, but I just can't do that... even tho that would have been REDICULOUSLY profitable to have done today. At one point, I did finally convince myself "Just throw a trade at it on your other brokerage account!" for my swing and hold account, because I was essentially monitoring the exact same trade. I really wish I would have done that for more but... coulda woulda shoulda would have ever one of us become billionaires! 

As for 11a... that's there also to protect ME from MYSELF with chasing more profits into the intraday trading, and likely giving up my morning gains. FAR too often have I gone spelunking for more profits, only to give up my entire mornings gains. So, unless I've got an A+ setup... and I'm just unable to resist entering... I let it go. Today was a GOOOOOOD reason to break the no trades after 11a rule!

author: Das Wookie
content: In the interest of sharing, I had an absolutely HORRIFIC trading day today. I lost my first two trades for the day, and should have followed my rule and walked away right then. Instead, I chased, and entered a trade without a proper setup entry JUST because it looked like it might be good. I sized WAY up to 400 contracts too try and make up for my loss. Turns out tho, there were NO options traders on that symbol. The Bid/Ask Spread was MASSIVE with a $3.50 spread, but I didn't notice until I went to exit. There were NO buyers. NONE!!! I pulled up the Time & Sales, and I thought the data connection was broken. I pulled up history, and I saw some trades of mostly 1 contact, a few for 2 contracts, a rare 5 or 10, and then my 400 contacts buy. There we no other options trades. I couldn't exit! Well I could, but with THAT spread, it was BRUTAL. I listed the limit sale at the ASK, and nothing happened for 30 seconds. I dropped my price by $0.10. Then again. And again. And again... and And AND!!! I finally sold my position after dropping my ASK $3.40 when it FINALLY sold. I went from being up and being green for the day, and turning that into a loss of $140k. I was DEVASTATED. I couldn't believe I got myself into that position. I found a stock that was moving, and did well... and destroyed myself. I did a few trades after that, but there was ZERO chance of being green. I was just TRYING to limit the damage. I ended up -$137k for the day. It's not the end of the world for me. I'm still green for the week, but it's embarrassing, emotional, and gravely stupid you have done. But it is what it is! So... There's my last 6 days of trading. It seems like people only ever post their gains or complain about losses. I traded emotionally. I chased. I kept increasing size to try and get back to even. I did ask if this on a 0DTE Friday of all days!!! I was, an idiot! So, I'm falling on my sword here.

author: DWalt Trades
content: Running hard stops is completely fine just always be sure to be aware of what the other time frames are shaping up like compared to the one you are trading on, and have a proper and defined risk. Jack and Tony emphasize the importance of a candle close so eventually you will get the grasp after enough wick outs then price running straight to TP, you will be able to identify how to avoid it in the future. I have saved my myself from fake invalidations and had very good trades play out by not letting a wick stop me, but I’ve also had some trades seriously run away by waiting for candle close. So It has its pros and cons and eventually, based on your own trading style, you will find out what works best for you and a lot of times it is based on situational context. But in the meantime I do strongly recommend a proper and defined risk level if you are running hard stops. I used to trade the 1M as well, but now I only enter and scale on it. My risk and target is always managed and defined on the 5 and 15 and its taken a lot of the unnecessary losses out, stopped me from entering fakeout/sweeps, and improved my win rate and trading overall.

author: DWalt Trades
content: I recommend doing trade review videos on all your trades, bar by bar in replay mode, considering more than one time frame, everyday after session. Along with mass backtesting sessions that are recorded. This will help you identify how and where to cut down the overtrading and hold your trades until they either are invalidated or objective filled. I say this because technically your first entry @ 25,798.50 was never invalidated, and your initial trade could’ve been held to main objectives instead of having multiple smaller trades inside of the initial main position. This could have been a one and done day. I used to find myself in trade reviews saying “I should have just held my initial entry” on a breakeven or small red day that could’ve very well been green days and ultimately those deep reviews are where my improvement came from. That is the main reason I responded to your first text in here about it because I immediately identified something that I used to fall victim to as well on your chart so any questions just tag me here and I am happy to help!

author: Humantorch
content: What's the minimum con amount where it starts to make sense to have trailers beyond your initial tp1/2?

author: Huy
content:
Seems like the average recommended position size to scale out is around 80% between TP 1 and 2. A few folks here would recommend only leaving a trailer if you have more than 5-6 cons but I personally think you can start with 3 for A+ set ups. Sell the first 2 cons early and leave the last one to see if it can ride the wave. The only downside is if the trade go against you then your gains from the first 2 cons will be wiped. Thats why I think 5 cons works better since you can sell 4 early then leave 1 to run. That 1 does not have enough weight to pull the prior profits if managed correctly. 

I am a big believer in actually doing. We can backtest / do simulation work / pretend how we scale in our minds etc but everything changes when you have real contracts and the tape is live. 

I just started to use this sheet here to help me quickly set my TP in the bracket order. The upside is I can lock in small consistent gains. The downside is I will miss on bigger moves on the first 80% of my position size. You can adjust the ATR to be more aggressive if you like which will change the values a bit.

let me know if you have any feedbacks!

author: Humantorch
content:
i dont take ATR into account so it's hard to comment on that aspect.
With 5 cons it's doable, but to be on safe side you would probably do 3-1-1 (3 HOD which is >50% of position then 1 con at TP1 and TP2) but depending on where the Key Levels are, and how you trade, may not be enough to be called a trailer position. 
With 6 cons, it starts to look better. 3 HOD(50%) then 1-1-1 if you want to catch bigger moves or 2-1 for safer approach.
WIth 7 cons i think this is where it starts to shine. 4HOD to have >50% of position secured then either 1-1-1 or 2-1. 
I feel like 7 is the ideal minimum, with 6 being a starter of what's possible. 
what do you think?

author: John H
content:
Quick post here regarding my oversized losses from Friday's session. I decided I would play WDC, and as we witnessed memory names had a brutal selloff. But more importantly what did I do wrong. I came in simply unprepared. WDC is not a ticker I normally trade. I had zero premarket prep. No key levels to watch and no exit plan when the trade turned against me. I failed to check a higher time frame to see that we were selling off. I should have marked off the open high low on the 5 min. candle as is often discussed here and I didn't do that either. So, an entire solid month of very good trading cost me more than 1/2 my gains for January. 

To make matters worse, I continued to trade. While I won most of those trades, I took yet another big loss on SNDK. I entered very late chasing more downside and the stock reversed near end of day. I'm saying this as a reminder to myself and for others that might be newer traders how very important it is to map out your game plan. To stay disciplined. To know your exit point if the trade turns against you. To not overtrade by taking too many trades, or revenge trade to try and recoup losses. Had I followed <@1014389514141184074> Friday advice I would of had a much better week & month. I was up $42K for January, I ended with $16K. I was disciplined all month long until Friday. That is truly how quickly you can make careless errors and give it all back. 

I still have a very high win rate, but you'll find that does not matter if you let trades get worse by the minute and don't get out when they are clearly not working. I will come to the market prepared, or I will not trade. That is something I have to follow for myself. We ride again Monday, and you all will see me turn this around. Best of luck everyone.

author: Joebag009
content:
The numbers: Cash account +64  Margin account:  -400
Take Profit Trader eval: -162 Top Step eval: -283 Apex evals: No trades

The Story
Lots of learning today. I’m not doing well personally, or in trading at the moment. Spilling my guts on here in a narrative others can comprehend instead of just short form writing to myself might help me work through it. I have done much better before so I know I can do this. 

Cash account: I have been trading in a margin account and sizing up worked great until my slump, since I have been trading 1-3 cons lately, I figured why not try a cash account with no margin? I took 720 bucks and put it in a cash account. I did one quick trade with JDUB on 2 0 day cons of NVDA and made 64 bucks. I could have held longer for continuation – I have gotten better, but still hold losers longer than winners. Losses happen faster than winners, cutting needs to be ruthless. I got lots of insight so my new rule for next week is trade 1 account only – cash if I can, margin if the trade of the day needs more capital. 

Margin account: I then had a thesis of continuation and a quick win in my margin account, but instead of waiting for a break and retest of that low of day on NVDA I entered too soon and it turned out being low of day. I stopped out on 2 NVDA 0 days for a loss of 314. Re-entering with hope of continuation and being wrong is something I am seeing as a problem. I will need to stick to the original setup and once I’m out, I’m out. If it costs me, I need to get better at exiting, instead of trying to re-enter. I also need to keep in mind 0 days move different. Looking at Fridays for the last 3 months I don’t see a meaningful performance difference from other days, so for now I’ll keep trading them.

author: Joebag009
content: I had a big AHA that if it was my cash account I never would have risked subsequent NVDA trades, so I think I might be trading my cash account with 720 in it (and taking profit out each day) for a few days to build my psychology up with a fresh start. Another big aha is that with 0s and even generally the first trade is the best trade and unless I have a new thesis or proved continuation (preferably with divergence from the market), re-entering is a bad idea - looking at my trading notes I have had wins in the past on re-entering for continuation, especially in big dumps around key levels and in grinds but this week it has not worked out (as in net profit) and eaten my meagre profits. Instead of blaming the market, I am adding new habits of pausing between trades and to reload I will develop a quick checklist for myself - did it defend a key level and continue or is it just teasing me, or is it just teasing me as it mirrors qqq moves (hey, it got Jack with TSLA this week, I need to be kind to myself, but also prevent it next time), is it over-extended on the stock, and with qqq overall? What is the daily tf trend? Are Jack and Tony still in and either not at target or just tp1? Tp2 and “to the moon” are horrible places for re-entry, even if it is holding a key level or 1 minute timeframe. Reminder to myself that re-entries pay less, fail more often, and lose money more quickly than initial setups.

Prop accounts: TPT -about 30 and 50 cents away from blowing it, will try a trade but fully expect to lose it and not planning to renew – will focus on my other accounts now that I have ruled out trading outside NY session.

Apex 02: +105 followed rules well
Apex 01: -264 slightly overtraded but caught myself and closed the charts before it got worse

Elite athletes don’t rise to the occasion; they fall to their level of training. -Neto Moreno

Theme of the day and Aha #1: SLOW THE HELL DOWN, be patient, DO NOT LONG OR SHORT IN CHOP, AT EDGE OF RANGES, AT HIGH OR LOW OF DAY… so in other words looking at a 1/5/15 min TF of a daily chart, the move looks HUGE, it makes us GREEDY, it makes us FEEL LIKE A BAD TRADER for “only” capturing xyz… but really, “perfect” is timing to SAFELY capture a relatively TINY part of that move with HIGH expectancy and CONSISTENCY. Doing it with 0s like JT (Jack and Tony) grants extra rewards.

Big aha for me today is how much more 0s can move compared to Friday expiries, 80% vs 20 profit roughly, however 0s are sooo spicy, even 1 option feels so high risk, definitely with the low discipline I have had lately. So, I could do 1 con 0 and be out when in profit, no re-entries ever on 0s, just with longer expiries, and not to mention that I’m super cooked if JT are wrong. I feel like I am at the beginning of my trading journey again, learning how to execute accurately and repeatedly with a single 0 will be a new challenge for me, I understand these are just for the main trade of the day and an A+ setup, I will want more wiggle room on most days until I’m consistently green.

author: Joebag009
content:
Big aha with FZ today is that just like with JT I overtraded and didn’t quite match his execution. My goal today was to trade less and more focused on his stops and setups. While I missed some of the moves and botched the execution a bit, I at least paid attention to him with a new type of mindset and followed through and am up in 1 out of two Apex accounts. I could feel my frontal cortex straining, doing REPS, as I breathed through my emotions and focused on market structure. I stayed OUT of MULTIPLE trades, that WOULD NOT HAVE WORKED. So that was a huge psych win for me today, which I need cause I feel like I’m on the cusp of either making it big or completely losing my mind. FZ did 3 trades today, I did more with less size, same pattern as with JT and re-entries, but a bit easier managed on the charts so less friction. 

12:30 PM Eastern behaviour of futures seems to change a bit and most consistent pros are done here if not a lot sooner. Big AHA: a price move on a chart is not equal, cons and futures move different depending on price volume and market structure. “d’uh” but seeing it in real time is different than seeing it in a lesson. A “choppy/grindy” move in both futures and contracts is a lot crappier than a clean smaller move with strong volume and direction. Again “d’uh”, but starting to see it is making a big difference in my emotions and execution. Also after 12:30 fills get worse on stopping out and taking profits making it harder to stack up wins that recover from even small losses (because bad fills make them even 3x worse than AM exit with the same distance stop). In other words, what the chart shows is a lie, the risks of extracting money from what you see go UP.

author: Joebag009
content:
Low of day to VWAP is a great secondary setup for futures, not usually for options as they move differently. Maybe I could capture that move on an index weekly, but I’m usually busy trading futures on that move. Important to note that into lunch and beyond sudden reversals from nowhere more likely, so hard stops needed on futures and not options friendly. 

You don’t need a strong reason to stay out, gut feeling is enough in addition to technicals, but you sure as shit need a strong reason to enter.

Old me got a reward from taking a trade as well as winning, new me is trying to stay neutral and get a reward from following rules. Trader vs gambler.

SORRY THESE POSTS ARE SO LONG... I'm not doing great right now, I will try to shorten these as I work through the challenges.

author: Porter
content:
I should have mentioned this earlier, but I did **not** intend to use the 5-minute timeframe for the entry not on this trade itself.

* The trade was meant to be a **break and retest of the pre-market level (PML)**.
* The **5-minute level** was only being used as a **price target** in this situation.
* At the time, the ticker was **below both the PML and the PDL**, with **very bearish price action** from the prior day.
* Because of that, I felt using the **PML as resistance** made sense.
* Price had **wicked into the EMA and VWAP twice and rejected**, which reinforced that bias.

I should also clarify:

* I trade primarily on the **3-minute chart**, not the 1-minute.
* That said, after dropping down to the **1-minute**, I see what you mean about the entry — and you’re right.

Looking back at it now using **TradingView replay**:

* The **9:36 candle on the 3-minute** was the correct entry.
* I entered **too late**, mainly due to hesitation on my part.
* There also wasn’t any real **displacement** at the point where I entered.

Honestly, the only way this trade would’ve worked — now that I think about it — was:

* Entering on the **second retest of the wick during the formation of the 9:36 candle**.

One question for you:

* When price is **below both the PML and the PDL**, do you **prioritize the 5-minute levels**, or is that **circumstantial** for you as well?
* And you’re absolutely right about **shorting at the key level**.

Sorry — ChatGPT helped me write this 😅

---

author: 305 Trader
content: one of the secrets of staying alive as an Options Trader, is to lose small. So I share with you a trick from observation. When you take an entry and the stock moves in your direction but wicks out a lot, this means there is still indecision. Do not get caught in that fight. Exit at small green or small red and wait for conviction from your Buyers. You can always reenter, if you were correct the market will go the way you needed and if it doesnt you were out already.

author: 305 Trader
content:
Someone told me that Hyenuk the Korean/Colombian trader who teaches in Spanish applies the rule of 8% of his contracts size for everything, either swings or intraday. This is so with slippage and your own reluctance to exit will never become more than 10% of your contract size that loss. People forget that we can always re-enter. Commissions are low, and unless you trade 50% of your account per trade, you can always re-enter. 
Never forget this, you can always re-enter. It took me almost 4 years to understand and apply this simple notion to become profitable. Sometimes, the obvious things are realized late and very very costly.

author: 305 Trader
content: Good morning. You were correct, the bottom was near but not quite at the time of your entry. It was at your exit.  Go in higher Time Frames, 4H and 1H with extended hours and find zones of solid confluence and draw them as rectangles as Jack does. Polish them in 15 mins TF around their edges. They will indicate bottoms and tops for larger moves. You need only few, maybe three max, 4. When the stock breaks those levels with a retest and making new higher lows (long) or new Lower highs (short) prior your entry, plus increasing volume and a solid reaction ( a hammer or a solid large bar),its a confirmation the reversal  or continuation is in place. This is how big moves are caught. If you only wait for this and do nothing, absolutely nothing else in your trading, you will transform your account. Then, study, study off time how the best break outs happen out of those levels and refine your entry. Go back in time in your Time frame of preference and one higher to study these breakouts from these zones you drew your zones exactly as I mentioned and study how the breakout took shape and what is the perfect set up for your entry. Last one, take this model and specialize in only one stock. Once you have dominated this (with $$$ to demonstrate it), you can add more stocks to your watch list. Of course, we often do what we want and think is correct, but if you would just listen to this advice, I promise you, you can get rich. This is exactly what I am doing now.

author: 305 Trader
content:
ok. Two things. One is from pure observation and keeping stats and the 2nd one is a recent development. 

-  when the adverse force prior your entry, is a dwindled and reduced candle, you can safely enter on your rising bar color which takes out that adverse coor bar. So then, to that extent you entered late. But, since the previous bars were evidently with long wicks it means, market indecision. So if you take an entry following those wicks, you have to off load heavy weight at the HOD or top of those wicks as an options trader. If futures, not as critical. 

- You have to have a thesis of the PM iin 1 HR tf if you are to enter in the firsr 30 mins of the market open, what was the structure formed? The price after the opening,  will revisit those PM levels and react strongly. Check on this in addition to the PD key levels.

author: Jatin
content:
You have already answered your questions. The contracts on NVDA move a lot less if you compare to AMD contracts. Just an example, if you are trading 4 cons in AMD, the usual risk is around $60 per contract. Say you lost that trade and on 4 contracts it will be -$240 on that trade.

Then you trade 4 contracts on NVDA, with usual risk being $30 per contract. Say you won this one and took a full exit at 2RR. Now you won and profit comes to $240 for your 4 contracts.

See now you are break even even after you took 2RR winner. The issue is sizing. On the first trade you were risking $240 but on second one you were risking $120.
To match the risk in the trades, you would have traded 8 contracts on NVDA with risking same $240 on the trade, now if you won at 2RR, you would have profitted $480

Let me know if this makes sense

author: DHayesTrades
content: I'm not very expereinced so please take my answer with a couple grains of salt - but I have learned over time that most people take 50% off at high of day or low of day as their first profit target. That is if the trade is a break and retest of PDH or PDL. If that target is a good ways aaway from your entry - take off 50% half way there, then take of 25-40% off at that target, then let e trailer run

author: Jatin
content:
<@1455167515926728907> <@1492137844276334827> What I have been following is very simple to follow. Helpful for small accounts, this will lead to more consistency than home run trades. Try to follow this:
If you have 1-3 cons, take full exit at 2RR. If you have 4 or more, scale out minimum 75% of position on 2RR and leave rest for next targets. This will make it simple to follow, focuses on consistent base hits instead of rare mooshots we aim for sometimes with greed or whatever.

The number of cons depend on the risk amount on cons for the trade

author: 305 Trader
content: Ok <@1490749230837399662> thanks for sharing. This is what I think it happened against you. - You did not allow a solid retest to the PDH to occur. This is a must at a very important key level such as PDH. If by waiting so the move takes off w/o you so be it. 2nd, Even if you waited for a solid retest to PDH as it happened few mins later in your chart, the PMH which was still not revisited by then, was quite close to your entry. So that dwindled further your shot at target of 1:2, for at best that entry was a 1:2 with zero error for the buyers. Wait up for larger space in your potential run, it will give you higher probs and also do not rush the retest, it has to be noticeable as per Hayden's explanations.

author: Torr
content:
The way I've managed this is two loses, I scale down for the day to half risk on everything. If I lose three in a row, I'm done. I only risk .5 R ever, so I only need 1.5 trades to go right to be back to green the next day. If I have a string of losing days (two in a row), I scale down at the start of the next day. Two green days, and I scale up again. My reasoning for this is that I'm losing either because 1. I don't have the edge I think I do (perhaps because market conditions have shifted without me knowing) 2. My head isn't right 3. I'm just hitting a losing streak, which is probalistic certainty in trading. Either of these options mean it's better to just scale back or stop. 

But I also get it that we get hijacked sometimes.

author: 👑 of Puts
content: I'd like to share few things. It's been 2 months in this room. And i had my attention glued to mainly learning. Taken probably about 10-15 trades in 2 months😵‍💫  Could have really followed the call outs and made money  🤌  But i can't take something i don't understand. Here's a few things that really helped: Creating a proper playbook with screenshots and entry/exit criteria; Backtesting each setup (proper backtesting, i realized i did it wrong all those years); doing video trade reviews (same as Tony and Jake do on youtube. created a channel and posting it there) Helps so much! Also created a discord where i post my watchlist and my thoughts. Having my thoughts written out helps with discipline!!! Some days i think i didn't make any progress so i made a list of things i learned in 2 months and it's impressive. Next step is getting off the lives and doing my own stream so i can talk through my thoughts and watch my emotions. Will review posted lives for my mistakes and thought process. I really need to hear my own thoughts otherwise i'm distracted. Some people can do 3 things at once, but it's not me. My main message here i guess i can't do less than Tony or Jake and be profitable.

author: Aleksandra Seliverstova
content: I have relevant problems, and I know some other people have these, because it's difficult to manage risk. If you enter with Tony/Jdub, it might be later than them because the video might lag behind. Thus stop loss is wider. Scaling out at HoD makes RR less. This might lead to losing the same amount on losing trades that you gain on winning trades (and no continuation on trailers). If you can't manage to risk each time the equal amount, which is hard because you don't always know the stop loss potential risk when you enter the trade, you might end up red.

author: Aleksandra Seliverstova
content:
Hi guys, this might be interesting to anyone struggling with RR like I do. I revised my trades (made during live sessions) of the last month, and here's the stats.

There were 10 losing trades (exit on SL, actually 9, but for one trade I couldn't remember the setup so I counted it as a loss in terms of this analysis), and 12 winning trades. 3 of the losses were on FOMC day, so they can actually be excluded as I shouldn't have taken them at all. But still.

RR of the winning trades: 
9,39
4,73
4,07
2,08
3,29
2,05
4,32
3,04
1,4
3,16
5,18
3,3
That's if I didn't close position until the initial stop loss was hit and just waited until the trend changes or the day ends, and took profit at maximum (which you never know for sure, of course).

So there was only one trade with RR less than 2.
I'm actually red this month due to uneven risk management and selling 30-50% of the position at HoD most of the times (I trade 4-6 cons).

So I made these calculations to understand what kind of result I would have now if I only sold at 2RR or 3RR.
Check the screenshot, it's all there. 
If you trade a small number of cons as I do you might come to the same conclusion as I did, that even closing the whole position at 3RR without taking partial scales at HoD/LoD might have been rather profitable.

Of course, that's a random month and these stats aren't really representative. But I personally want to try something like selling 4 cons out of 6 at 2RR, and the rest (2) at 3RR.

Hope this is helpful 🙌

author: Aleksandra Seliverstova
content:
I never moved my SL to BE, I just scaled out 30-50% at HoD/LoD and scaled out the rest at psychological numbers and final target without any trailers. This led me to playing most of the time at RR 1:1, if not worse. That's why I decided to check the actual potential RR of the trades I took.

As for the analysis, you can see above that of my 9 losing trades I could restore, only 1 could use scaling out at HoD/LoD, and the rest of losing trades never reached HoD/LoD. So, if of my 12 winning trades I scale all of them 50% at HoD/LoD, that's 50% loss of potential profit of 12 trades, compared to this one trade where kinda saved 1/2 of risk by scaling out at HoD/LoD.

Of my trades of this month, many times price was kinda consolidating right after the entry, so closing position at BE didn't make any sense most of the time

Like this trade on AAPL I took on August 19th. Most of the time it's like this. The price is indecisive and then it breaks up or down. If I cut the position at BE, considering the entry price, the RR just doesn't make sense.

author: demchy19
content: Based on how the market moved and the pre-market setup, I was looking to trade scalps, focusing on retests and then targeting the PDH. For NVDA, my strategy was to enter after it broke out of the PMH. On the 1-minute chart, it showed a good displacement and then retested the PMH, where I entered with 6 contracts. I sold 3 contracts at the HOD, 2 contracts at halfway to the PDH, and the last contract once it broke below the PHOD. I didn't expect it to move too far but was aiming for just the PDH. But after it broke the PDH somehow was really weak compare to overall market (QQQ). I don't understand why. But I still saw a good trade.  Even with the AMZN trade, it was an intraday trade, and it didn’t have higher time frame thesis. The final target was the PDH. This is how I understand the situation and Jack’s approach.

author: Lane 🌿
content: I traded on my phone when I was a welder at a plant (super fast paced and strenuous) and had some success. I used WeBull because the mobile app is very very good and you can actually get in and out pretty quick. I bought one of those kickstand cases for my phone and I would put it in my welding cabinet where no bosses or anything could see. I kept 1 earbud in and had my sound on and notifications set on my charts for certain levels. Like for example the 5m range established so I'd go ahead and put an alert for above/below those levels by 1 cent. When they broke I'd get alerted, and when they'd come back to retest I'd get another alert! So I'd run over real quick and take my gloves off and get in the trade. Then I'd set an alert 10-15 cents above my stop so I'd get alerted and could stop out if needed. I'd have alerts 5 cents lower than each profit target to give myself time to get to my phone for the pop so I could scale out. I did this for about a year solid. It can be stressful, but once you kind of figure out your little way to do it and whatnot it can be done! Hope this helps man.

author: MVP Freestyle
content: Choose options that expire 3 months out and swing trade it for a one to a few days. Take a contract slightly out of the money and let that appreciate to target. When you see perfect setup within that position buy a weekly contract to add  leverage to position. Essentially you are using the 3 month contract as a placeholder to add additional leverage on a shorter weekly timeframe when conditions are a no brainer.

author: demchy19
content:
Hi Lauren,
I'm not a professional trader yet; I'm still learning, but I'll share my thoughts on your question. I don't think this follows the 84% rule. If I understand correctly, the 84% rule applies when you have a plan for entering a stock at a specific key level (e.g., the 5-minute opening candle print), and the stock tries to retest that level but fails and goes lower. The 84% rule comes into play when the stock returns to that key level and breaks through it. At this point, you just watch the price reaction at that level. Typically, with the 84% rule, the stock doesn't retest the key level—it just breaks through and moves in the right direction.
From the picture below, we can see that the stock has retested the same level twice. This can give you a stronger confirmation that the level is well established and could push the stock lower. There are likely many sellers above this level, preventing the stock from going higher, so there's a good chance it will drop. But based on your take profit and stop loss, you seem to be in a good position.

author: Angel (az)
content:
I can't speak for the pros, but here is my personal experience as a beginner - I'm able to hold the trade and not care because I am comfortable with the risk im exposed to at 2 cons total, I am comfortable with it dropping 50% in value overnight in the worst case. And 2 is easier to manage than 1 IMHO. I don't know how you're sizing but it needs to be what you personally are comfortable with mentally at this point in your journey. I am strict with scaling out at the first pop up which gives you realised gains and it's like a cheat code that makes it easier for you psychologically to hold the second one. The Tesla trade this week for example, my first con scale was 300+ realised, so now psychologically, i am okay with my second con going to -300. (I would not wait that long to stop out, but it lets you breathe and gives you a lot of room mentally). Ever since then, I literally try to not even look at the value of my second con or the price, I am just waiting for the HTF target to hit or structure to break. 

If the trade doesn't work: I believe Jack or Tony said you have your stop losses as usual in terms of trades not going your way, but you have to be more comfortable with a larger percentage drawdown. Stop loss is at where key HTF levels and structures are broken

author: Angel (az)
content: Np 🙂 stay as long as the market dictates since we can't predict when or if it'll actually happen. You just take it day by day and stay in your trailer con until 1) HTF take-profit target hits or 2) structure breaks, and stop loss hits. Case 3 I imagine is expiration day hits and neither 1 nor 2 happened, in which case I would probably reevaluate whether everything still looks solid, and close this week's and decide whether to buy next week's expiration. (Have to check with others on this).

author: Angel (az)
content: Oh and in case 1, if there are no more HTF levels then you start monitoring the chart real time and have to use more discretion/experience when to close out your last trailers. I would probably try to make myself a rule that's more mechanical since I still lack that experience. So probably something like - I must take profit after first bearish candle closes after ATH, or, after first close under an order block after ATH. That way I have a clear exit plan while I hopefully eventually gain that experience

content: Two Trades today, NVDA. Traded the retest of 5 min Low to the upside. author: BrokeAintNoJoke
content:
I took the put trade yesterday because of multiple confluences. Price had retraced to the 61% fib level which correlated closely to the KL from previous PDH (I read trading bible over last weekend in books channel), PA was showing wicks up for a few bars showing sellers keeping price from breaching, there was a perfect test of the 1min ORB low and I think at the same time Qs and Spy showed weakness. 
I did not expect it to go down that quickly and exited all cons in the same fashion missing out on the huge move downward. I failed to leave trailers and scale. Also exited completely because just minutes prior I was in a call that at peak was 3x what I closed scaling 25% then 75% at breakeven. One lesson is I need to remember to leave trailers. Missed the big move downward. could have been a $3k day instead of $550 if I left trailers. That said I am happy that I am starting to see profitable entries.

author: StevenP
content:
ORB-Opening range break
OB- Order Block
PDL- Prev day low
PDC- Prev day close
PMH/PML pre market high/low
HOD-High of day
LOD- Low of day
You can make a list of the ones you don’t know and ask here as well.

author: StevenP
content:
ORB-Opening Range Break 
OB-Order Block
PDL-Previous Day Low 
PDC-Previous Day Close 
PMH-Pre Market High
PML-Pre Market Low 
HOD-High Of Day
LOD-Low Of Day
HTF-Higher Time Frame 
LTF-Lower Time Frame
BNR-Break And Retest
SL-Stop Loss
TP-Take Profit
ATH-All Time Highs
TSL-Tight Stop Loss
BOS-Break Of Structure 
CHoCH-CHange Of CHaracter
MSS-Market Structure Shift

author: Moni Leine
content:
Hey Traders,
@everyone
Another solid session in the books with our coach JDub! Here’s what we covered today:

🔥 Weekly Trades Recap
Reviewed key setups and how they were executed.

📌 Entry Models
Conservative
Most Conservative
Aggressive

⏳ Timeframes & Execution
Thesis is built on 15’ charts to identify key levels.
Entries are executed on the 1’ chart for precision.

📍 Drawing Levels
Levels are drawn on the 15’ timeframe to track price action.
Targets are adjusted based on liquidity levels.

📰 JDub’s Pre-Market Prep
1️⃣ News Analysis – Understanding market-moving events.
2️⃣ HTF Market Context – Evaluating trends on higher timeframes.
3️⃣ A+ Setups Identification – Looking for high-probability setups based on:
PDH BNR (Previous Day High Break and Retest)
PDL BNR (Previous Day Low Break and Retest)
OR BNR (Opening Range Break and Retest)

📈 Risk & Position Management
Jack’s risk plan – How he manages trades on 1’ and 5’ timeframes.
Sizing Decisions – Adjusting position sizes for different trade setups.
"Look Left" Strategy – Define targets based on liquidity levels.

📊 How to Scale Out of a Trade
Scaling out in stages helps lock in profits while letting winners run. Here’s the approach (with an example, but you can set your own scaling plan):
1️⃣ First Exit – Secure Partial Profits (25%)
Sell 25% at the first key level to lock in profits.
2️⃣ Second Exit – Reduce Risk (25%)
Sell another 25% as price moves further in your favor.
3️⃣ Third Exit – Maximize Gains (25%)
Sell 25% when price hits a major breakout level or extension target.
4️⃣ Final Exit – Let the Runner Go (25%)
Let the rest run using the bar by bar method (trail stop with each new bar on the 1’ timeframe).

Thanks for joining! These sessions are packed with actionable insights—keep showing up, refining your skills, and taking advantage of these learning opportunities.

📅 See you next time! 🚀

author: NickA
content: joined only 1 week ago... Copying them straight up has given me winners and losers.... but you have to handle your own risk/sizing/etc.  Try to make it about the process and evolving away from copying T and J to following along to learn the decision making and flow... easier said than done...  improve scanning technique on multiple symbols and id'ing the key levels with them....  study their pre-market strategy and real-time thought processes and adjustments... it's game changing for me...  seeing them in action helps me modulate my trading frequency and build a better eye for hitting better trades.   Still trying come up with my own actionable trades with better entry/exit plans every day and calming down in general.  Using their actions and comments as a "confluence" but not the only reason to jump in a trade is the pattern I am trying to break.... I am gaining more comfort with the idea of doing it solo. Still so much growth required (and accelerator training) but it's starting to slow down and feel more settled thanks to both T, J, and the community.

author: DarthTrader
content: I base it off the underlying technical level. I do the math on how much I want to risk per contract and take it accordingly. I actually scale into my position so my initial position isn't huge and my losses are smaller as most trades fail in the first few min if they don't go right away. Once the make a new high or low, I actually add to my position (full) then adjust SL as needed. By this time usually we are breaking the highs/low and retesting. If that fails then break even it is. If not we are good.

author: Nines
content:
some food for thought for you guys; 

scaling out or taking partials are for those who dont trade the bigger picture. reason u never see me scaling out a contract and holding to my tp because it skews my RR significantly. instead of making 6R ill make only 2.5R or lower. then you wonder why your losses are as big as your winners or even bigger. 

with option its tricker because you almost have to scale out because greeks will kill you if you dont, but if you trade futures or forex i recommend you hold to your tp because the days you lose which you will, it wont hurt because your constantly catching 5R trades on the regular. 

im not saying this to bash on anyone who scales out if that works for you and you have success doing that then keep doing it but for me in my experiene i used to see my losers were as big as my winners even tho i was catching 6R trades but when i would scale out at hod or a random level i had marked it skewed with my RR

author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)
content:
When Walmart WMT releases its earnings, it often includes commentary from executives about consumer behavior—such as shifts in discretionary spending, preferences for value-driven purchases, or e-commerce growth—which can signal broader economic trends. 
Given Walmart's size and competition with companies like Amazon and Target, these insights can influence investor perceptions of those retailers as well, often leading to movements in their stock prices. 
For example, if Walmart reports strong sales and optimistic guidance, it might boost confidence in the retail sector, lifting stocks like Amazon or Target. Conversely, a weak report or cautious outlook could raise concerns about consumer spending, pressuring those stocks.
It's not a separate report, it's inside the ER.

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content:
<@402084107951144973>  - 

🔍 1-Min Chart (Left Side)
More sensitive / reactive to short-term price movements.

The “Protect Your Asset” trendline on the 1-min chart is drawn tighter and shows earlier signs of reversal.

This chart captured a break of the downtrend earlier, showing bullish momentum forming right after Scale #3.

Best for quick scalps or precise entry/exit signals if you're managing a trade closely.

Shows micro price action and short-term exhaustion or trend shift faster.

⏱️ Use case: Ideal for day traders wanting to lock in profits quickly or protect positions once momentum shifts.


 5-Min Chart (Right Side)
Less sensitive and shows more confirmed structure.

The “Protect Your Asset” trendline here is wider and slower, but gives a stronger trend confirmation.

The trendline broke after Scale #3, confirming a larger shift in trend on a higher timeframe.

Gives more trustworthy signals when looking for bigger moves or validating a trend reversal.

Smoother and filters out noise compared to the 1-min.

⏱️ Use case: Ideal for swing entries, trailing stops, or holding positions longer with more confidence.

author: GoodVibez
content: Hey. Firstly, I feel your pain man. Gotta look at those higher time frames. Pretty much what Jack/Tony did. But remember they're not always correct in the immediate sense. Remember that huge TSLA swing  win Tony had. His orignial entry wasn't that great. It retraced down like $18. He bought more to offset while it worked back to original target. I'm not saying do that. Buy what did you see on higher timeframe that inspired you to enter Shorts? Also be mindful you only have until the June 6. Theta is gonna rip into the liquidity esp. as it sits negative. My experience...patience is good when on the the right side of a trade OR when you're intitally wrong, trust your thesis, and have bought enough time out (like 2 weeks to a month) for you to profit or at least break even. Maybe give it a lil time but you also don't wanna regret not cutting your losses at 3k when you're down $12k. Also think about the consolidation on the Daily chart. So if we're breaking above this level... it's probably gonna move with a little strength. very unlikely those puts will recover.

author: GoodVibez
content: At what Price point did you enter your shorts? This is something that I've kept in mind as I've experience a few big hit and have seen some comrades take some massive hits. This one thing... the market is predisposed to go higher. It's nature. We plant seeds so they can grow. Big Institutions want more, not less. So moves down aren't beneficial in the broader picture. I only enter puts in the short-term sense. IF it hits my stop loss or doesn't move towards my target with immediate conviction... I'm out that trade. I also don't leverage as much when entering shorts. Holding shorts for swings is a no-go in my rule book. I wait for big retracements or breakout levels and buy UP for time out. The likelihood your cons recover with longs/calls is higher. So the general consensus...CUT YOUR LOSSES, before the losses become too large for you to recover from. Can't recover if you don't have any funds to recover with. And the longer you wait... the more theta will eat your liquidity.

author: Brute7
content:
It’s officially been one month since I started my intraday trading journey — and it all began with inspiration from the Scarface YouTube channel. What started as simple curiosity quickly turned into a daily commitment to grow, learn, and show up — regardless of profits or losses.

Like many beginners, I dove into breakout, retest, and price action strategies, thinking I had a solid handle on options trading. But over time, I realized there’s so much more to it — market news, economic reports, index correlations, relative strength, option Greeks, and key levels all play a critical role. One of my early mistakes was spreading my attention too thin, tracking too many tickers without focusing deeply on any.

So three days ago, I made a shift: I committed to just five tickers — SPX, NVDA, TSLA, QQQ, and SPY. Since then, I’ve seen a clear difference. Studying these names daily has helped me understand their behavior better, enter trades with more precision, and exit with more confidence.

Today, I hit my personal all-time high in trading. It’s not $10k (yet), but I know I’m on the right path — one day at a time.

author: Kam
content:
Hey Viper - this is definitely a loaded question lol.
Hard to answer without seeing more of your data or trading patterns, but here are a few thoughts that might help:

**Plan your levels + scenarios**
If you don’t have a mapped path for where 3R–5R could come from (HTF levels, gaps, reclaim zones), it’s hard to stay in the trade. Build the scenario before you enter.

**Revisit the HOD scale**
If you're trading a small number of cons and scaling at 1R/HOD, you're likely choking the trade by taking a large % off before the trade really gets moving. That said, don’t throw out the HOD rule blindly -  let structure & context guide 

**Mind your strike**
Pay attention to where underlying price is relative to your strike when you hit HOD. A lot of the juice in options comes from delta expansion past the strike - not just tagging it.

**Trailer logic **
Even something simple like bar-by-bar, 9EMA, or last HL break helps you stay in runners. Without a plan, it's easier to flatten early.

**Journaling & Expectations**
Which setups actually run 3-5R+? Even if you scaled out early, make sure you're tracking which trades run. Not all of them do. Also keep in mind that even if the max R is 5R, most likely you won't be able to capture the full 5R due to scaling into pops. I try to aim for 50-60% "R capture" as a metric I track regularly. 

Let me know if you want to walk through a specific trade. feel free throw one up in <#1219021113686888478>  and tag me - happy to dive in.

author: Hayden
content:
- Candle Close Stop Loss

Pros:

Filters out “wick hunts” and intraday noise.

Keeps you in trades that recover quickly after a fake-out.

Helps align with structure rather than random ticks.

Cons:

Risk can be larger than expected, since by the time the candle closes, the option premium may be down much more.

Hard to execute in options intraday because option premiums are extremely sensitive (gamma, delta shifts). A stock moving $0.30 against you can crush the premium more than expected.

Can lead to hesitating, hoping the candle “saves” the trade, which sometimes just means deeper loss.



- Hard Stop at Exact Price

Pros:

Clear, mechanical, no decision fatigue.

Protects capital better, especially in options where theta + gamma magnify losses quickly.

Removes emotional “what if it comes back?”

Cons:

You’ll get wicked out more often, especially intraday.

Slippage can hurt — a 10–20¢ slip in stock can translate into 20–30% option loss.

Misses some trades that recover immediately after the fake-out.



- Slippage Reality in Options

Options don’t move smoothly with the underlying; spreads + illiquidity + IV shifts = exits are rarely clean.

Market orders at stop get filled worse than stock.

Sometimes you get slipped on size even if spreads look tight.

This is why many intraday options traders actually prefer mental stops or alerts and exit manually, because hard stops can trigger at the worst possible premium.


Simplification: If you hesitate a lot on your stops, you have a small account, use exact stop levels. (Manual or Orders) The BNR usually works right away or it doesn't at all so most of the time (In my opinion) its worth it to close out. Tony and Jack have a lot higher risk tolerance than most and have been trading price action for many years.

If you wait for closures - accept the risk you're R/R might be skewed on a lot of your trades but your win % might be higher (if they reverse), better for experienced traders

author: Jerry
content:
Hey guys,

Wanted to share my experience of going from struggling to profitable (just a few months so far, but still). Maybe it can be a good starting point for some of you—it really changed the game for me.
I struggled a lot before. At one point, I was trading 20 TSLA contracts, taking small wins but then wiping everything out with big losses. Ended up down almost $15k in just a few months. That’s when I stopped and decided to really think.
I built an Excel table with all of Tony’s trades this year, split by symbol. Every row was a trade with entry (I added some delay since I’m slower), stop loss, max potential win until SL was hit, etc. I kept adding parameters and I’m still revisiting them, improving execution.
From that analysis, I found the main thing that killed my profitability: scaling out too early. I used to sell 30% on the HOD pop, then more on the next big candle, and my RR was completely ruined.
So I made a script in Excel to simulate different scenarios and find the best monthly outcome. The result was simple: don’t scale at all until TSLA moves at least $4 (around 2R), then exit. That’s it.
That one change completely flipped my trading. Now I trade 10 contracts and make way more than I did before with 20. This is just the first step, of course. Next is working on dynamic sizing based on setup quality, scaling in/out properly, etc. But this shift was the key—it stopped the bleeding and got me back to making money.

Hope it will help

author: Luke
content:
Since they're 0DTE contracts I play them a lot differently than I would futures contracts.
a) I'm still developing and refining my playbook, but what I usually do is trail it by the break of structure on the 1min after a decent move where the contracts are at least 15-20%. When there is a flush and the contracts start to move, market structure starts to really play out and be respected.
b) On these setups a lot of times when it consolidates during the trade and starts to stall and not move down, there can be really impulsive green candles which crush the contracts. They're usually easy to spot within the first few seconds of the candle because buyers aggressive start stepping in. In that case I'll usually stop out once candle just gets above my invalidation level. What I'm trying to work on right now is not letting green trades go red.
Hopefully that makes sense let me know if it does.

Yeah. Doing your own analysis is essential. Only that way you will get confidence. I simulated different appproaches.
1. No scaling. That gives the best results - best RR and 50% win rate
2. 1 scale. Again tried to find an optimum with simulation where and how many to scale to improve win rate at the price of RR. Managed to do it. Increased to 65% win rate with some combinations but it hit the total profit.
3. 1 scale + runners. Same results as in second approach.

So after all simulations no scaling approach gives the best profit, though much more complicated from mental perspective. 

To the question why Tony manages to have such great result - i see 3 points
1. Much better entry so good RR on HOD
2. Scaling based on good reading of price action like scaling out when several wicks as a sign of weakness
3. Scaling out base on context and not on predefined structure.

But all these points come with experience. We will get there someday
